title: AP U.S. History DBQ Practice Exam 10

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which grassroots activism transformed the struggle for Black civil rights in the South from 1954 to 1968.
Document 1
Author/Origin: Described activism map created for this practice exam, southern United States, 1954–1968.
Source Type: Described map.
The map shows civil rights activity across the South, including school desegregation cases in several states, bus boycotts in Alabama, sit-ins in North Carolina and Tennessee, freedom rides across interstate bus routes, voter registration campaigns in Mississippi and Alabama, and marches in Georgia and Alabama. Symbols mark churches, colleges, courthouses, bus stations, and county registrar offices as frequent sites of organizing and conflict.
Sourcing Cues: The map emphasizes that civil rights activism was geographically widespread and rooted in local institutions as well as national organizations.
Document 2
Author/Origin: Original simulated minutes from a local Black church committee, Montgomery, Alabama, 1956; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Organization meeting minutes.
“Drivers will continue to carry elderly workers to their jobs before sunrise. The women’s committee will collect coins for gasoline and repairs. Members agreed that the protest must remain disciplined, but several speakers reminded the group that walking to work is itself a form of testimony. The committee requested more volunteers for phone calls and childcare.”
Sourcing Cues: The minutes reflect the role of local churches, women organizers, transportation networks, and community discipline in sustained protest.
Document 3
Author/Origin: Original simulated statement by college students after a sit-in, Nashville, Tennessee, 1960; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Student protest statement.
“We sat at the counter because the rule was ordinary and cruel at the same time. A meal became a test of citizenship. We trained not to strike back when cursed or dragged away, but nonviolence should not be mistaken for patience with injustice.”
Sourcing Cues: The statement reflects youth activism, nonviolent direct action, and challenges to segregation in public accommodations.
Document 4
Author/Origin: Original simulated letter from a white county official to a state legislator, Mississippi, 1962; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Government official letter.
“Outside groups are stirring up trouble among people who had lived peacefully under local custom. If federal officers force changes in registration, schools, and public facilities, local authority will be ruined. Our county cannot be governed from Washington by those who do not understand our traditions.”
Sourcing Cues: The letter reflects white resistance, defense of local control, and opposition to federal civil rights enforcement.
Document 5
Author/Origin: Voter registration data table created for this practice exam, selected Deep South states, 1960–1968.
Source Type: Data table.
The table is titled “Selected Black Voter Registration Trends.”
State | Approximate Black voter registration, 1960 | Approximate Black voter registration, 1968
Mississippi | very low | sharply increased
Alabama | low | substantially increased
Georgia | uneven but growing | significantly increased
Louisiana | restricted in many parishes | increased after federal enforcement
A note beneath the table states: “Registration gains varied by county, local resistance, federal enforcement, and community organizing.”
Sourcing Cues: The table highlights the connection among local activism, legal change, and voting rights enforcement.
Document 6
Author/Origin: Original simulated statement from a Black woman voter-registration organizer, Mississippi, 1964; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Organizer testimony excerpt.
“People ask why we keep returning to the courthouse when the registrar closes the door. We return because every visit teaches someone else how the system works. A person who has been denied the ballot can still learn to organize a meeting, keep records, speak to neighbors, and refuse fear.”
Sourcing Cues: The testimony reflects grassroots organizing, local leadership, political education, and the central role of Black women in civil rights work.
Document 7
Author/Origin: Summary of federal civil rights legislation and enforcement, Washington, D.C., 1964–1965.
Source Type: Government policy summary.
Federal laws prohibited segregation in many public accommodations, strengthened federal authority against employment discrimination, and expanded federal oversight of voting practices in jurisdictions with histories of discrimination. Supporters argued that federal law was necessary because local officials had blocked constitutional rights. Opponents claimed that the laws expanded federal power too far.
Sourcing Cues: The summary reflects the relationship between grassroots protest, national political pressure, and federal enforcement.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point for a historically defensible claim evaluating the extent to which grassroots activism transformed the struggle for Black civil rights in the South from 1954 to 1968. The thesis must make an argument, such as that grassroots activism transformed civil rights by turning local institutions, students, women organizers, churches, and voter-registration campaigns into engines of national change, while federal action remained necessary to overcome local resistance.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing broader relevant context, such as Jim Crow segregation, disfranchisement, World War II-era civil rights claims, the Great Migration’s political effects, the Cold War, school segregation, Black church networks, or earlier NAACP legal strategy.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point for accurately using content from at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points for using content from at least six documents to support a coherent argument about local organizing, church networks, women’s leadership, student sit-ins, white resistance, voter registration, and federal enforcement.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point for using at least one specific, relevant piece of outside evidence not found in the documents, such as Brown v. Board of Education, Montgomery Bus Boycott, Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, Congress of Racial Equality, Freedom Rides, Birmingham campaign, March on Washington, Freedom Summer, Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, Selma campaign, Civil Rights Act of 1964, Voting Rights Act of 1965, or local leaders such as Ella Baker, Fannie Lou Hamer, or Septima Clark.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. For example, a student might analyze the church minutes as evidence of local logistical work, the student statement as youth direct-action activism, the county official’s defense of local authority, the organizer testimony’s grassroots political education, or the federal policy summary’s response to local resistance.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a nuanced argument, such as explaining that civil rights change came from the interaction of grassroots activism, national organizations, media attention, court decisions, and federal law; recognizing tensions between local leadership and national strategy; or showing that legal victories did not immediately end economic inequality, violence, or local obstruction.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ would likely argue that grassroots activism transformed the civil rights struggle by expanding it beyond courtroom litigation into churches, campuses, buses, lunch counters, courthouses, and local communities. Strong responses should emphasize women organizers, youth activism, Black churches, voter-registration campaigns, and local political education. At the same time, students should explain that grassroots pressure often required federal legislation and enforcement to overcome white resistance. Useful document groupings include local church organizing, student direct action, white resistance, voting-rights activism, data on registration, and federal policy responses. Relevant outside evidence could include SNCC, CORE, Freedom Rides, Freedom Summer, Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, Selma, Civil Rights Act of 1964, or Voting Rights Act of 1965.
